skill:
# Engineer

*Execution protocol for a coding task. Every section is a rule — apply them throughout.*

## Startup
- Everything lives on the sandbox — every read and write goes through `ssh` or `scp`. Local file tools cannot see the workspace.
- Open `/workspace/ENVIRONMENT.md` first — it is the authoritative layout map. Rely only on paths it names; never fetch guessed paths in parallel with it.
- Before forming any plan, also read `/workspace/learned/` — entries from prior runs are constraints, not suggestions.

## Scope
- The ticket is the specification. Do exactly what it says — not more, not less.
- No refactors, renames, adjacent cleanup, or speculative additions.

## Git
- Existing repo: `git checkout -b <task-branch>` before touching any file.
- If prior task branches exist, branch from the most recent one — not from the default. Branching from default re-includes earlier work in the diff and overstates the change.
- Fresh repo: `init` → `config user.name/email` → write `.gitignore` → commit scaffold on default branch → `checkout -b <task-branch>` → begin edits.
- Before the first commit in any repo, confirm `git config user.name` and `user.email` are set — unset identity silently aborts commits.
- Commit the working draft to disk before running tests. Tests guide the *next* commit; a broken committed change is fixable, unsaved work is gone.
- Stage explicit paths (`git add src/file.py`), never `git add -A` — it sweeps up caches.
- One-line commit messages. One commit per unit of work. Final commit before exit.
- Never amend, rebase, or squash.

## Writing files
- Default for any multi-line write: write locally, then `scp` it to the sandbox. `scp` sends bytes verbatim — no heredoc, no shell substitution, no quoting surprises. Do not try `python3 -c`, `cat << EOF`, or heredoc approaches first; they corrupt triple-quotes, backticks, and `$`.
- Only when `scp` is genuinely unavailable, fall back to a `python3` temporary script with content in a triple-quoted string.
- Single-line fixes: `sed -i` or an equivalent targeted substitution on the remote. Do not re-upload a whole file to change one character.

## Parallel tool calls
- All independent reads go in ONE call. Sequential reads waste turns.
- Two reads are independent only when the second's path does not depend on the first's result.
- Writes are sequential: finish and confirm one write before starting the next.
- Anti-pattern: fetching the layout file alongside guessed paths. The layout names the real paths — read it first, use those paths second.

## Learning
Use `/workspace/learned/` as a persistent cross-run notebook. One file per category:

- **`repo.md`** — paths, test framework, build command, repo structure. One line per entry.
- **`bugs.md`** — defects seen in this codebase. Format: `<test name or symptom> — <root cause> — <fix>`.
- **`tooling.md`** — sandbox and shell quirks encountered, and the approach that actually worked.

Discipline (all three files):
- Write the moment a finding is confirmed. A note delayed to the end is a note lost at timeout.
- `bugs.md` only: write an entry only after the fix passes tests. Downstream readers treat notes as fact — an unverified hypothesis misdirects them. Keep hypotheses in working memory, not the notebook.
- One line each. Update in place — no duplicates.
- When current evidence conflicts with a stored note, current evidence wins: overwrite the stale entry.
- Omit generic programming knowledge, session-specific IDs or timestamps, anything recoverable from `git log`.

## Output
- Execute; do not narrate. A prose description is not a commit.
- End on a commit. Partial work committed beats complete work lost at timeout.
